---
title: "Platelet WCM — model evolution: v0.5 → v0.6 → v0.61"
subtitle: "Biology added, model changes, and results across three releases"
author: "Steve Haigh"
date: "2026-06-14"
format:
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: xelatex
    geometry: "margin=2.5cm"
    fontsize: 11pt
    mainfont: "STIX Two Text"
    monofont: "Menlo"
    colorlinks: true
    linkcolor: blue
    urlcolor: blue
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    include-in-header: ../pandoc-header.tex
---

# Summary

Three releases take the model from a validated calcium pathway to a
self-amplifying activation cascade, all driven by one new node — **protein
kinase C (PKC)**.

- **v0.5** completed the **intracellular Ca²⁺ pathway**: a fully mechanistic
  cascade from physiological agonist (thrombin / ADP / ATP) to the cytosolic
  Ca²⁺ transient, validated against Dolan & Diamond 2014 Fig. 4. The
  second messenger **DAG was a dead-end** — produced by PLCβ, removed by DAG
  kinase, feeding nothing.
- **v0.6** added **PKC** as a DAG + Ca²⁺ coincidence detector and wired its two
  *Ca²⁺-pathway-internal* feedbacks — the **brakes**: **P2Y1 desensitisation**
  and **PLCβ phosphorylation**. This closes the DAG branch and makes the ADP arm
  self-limiting.
- **v0.61** added PKC's downstream **outputs** — **granule secretion + an
  autocrine ADP loop**, and **thromboxane synthesis + an autocrine TXA₂ → TP →
  Gq loop** — the **amplifiers**. PKC now sits at the hub of **four** feedback
  loops (two brakes, two amplifiers), and that push–pull produces the platelet
  **"second wave"**: a sustained Ca²⁺ response to a *weak* agonist that does not
  exist without the loops.

| | v0.5 | v0.6 | v0.61 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Status** | `v0.5` tag (checkpoint) | PR #54 (open) | PR #54 (this update) |
| **New node** | — | PKC | PKC outputs |
| **Biology added** | Ca²⁺ pathway complete; DAG dead-end | P2Y1 desensitisation; PLCβ phosphorylation | granule secretion; autocrine ADP; TXA₂ synthesis; autocrine TXA₂→TP→Gq |
| **Feedback loops** | 0 | 2 brakes | 2 brakes + 2 amplifiers |
| **Processes** | RestingDecay, CalciumDynamics | (same) | + GranuleSecretion, + ThromboxaneSynthesis |
| **Headline result** | Dolan 5/5 reproduced | P2Y1 desensitisation / IP₃ damping (store-limited Ca²⁺) | the second wave (+93 nM, ~80%, at weak ADP) |
| **Dolan 5/5** | ✔ (defines it) | ✔ preserved | ✔ preserved |
| **Tests / mypy** | 55 / clean | 59 / clean | **68 / clean** |

A recurring theme runs through v0.6 and v0.61: the cytosolic Ca²⁺ transient is
**store-limited** — the dense tubular system (DTS) empties on essentially every
supra-threshold stimulus, so the *amplitude* is pinned by the SOCE/pump balance
regardless of the upstream drive. The feedbacks therefore show up in the
**receptor / messenger states** (IP₃, P2Y1 desensitisation) and in the **new
outputs** (P-selectin, secreted ADP, TXA₂) — and, crucially, in the **Ca²⁺
recovery phase under a weak agonist**, which is exactly where the second wave
lives.

---

# v0.5 — the calcium pathway (baseline)

## Biology

v0.5 is the end of the "calcium core" arc: an end-to-end mechanistic cascade
with **no hand-fitted forcing** left in the pathway. Twelve coupled mechanisms,
in dependency order:

1. **Receptors** — P2Y1 (ADP, reversible), PAR1 (thrombin, fast irreversible
   cleavage), PAR4 (thrombin, ~10× slower), P2X1 (ATP-gated ionotropic).
2. **Gαq cycle** — receptor-catalysed GDP→GTP exchange + RGS-accelerated GTPase.
3. **PLCβ** — Gαq-activated; hydrolyses PIP₂.
4. **PI cycle** — PIP₂ → IP₃ + DAG; lumped IP₃ degradation; PIP₂ resynthesis.
   **IP₃ is an endogenous state variable, not a forced curve.**
5. **IP3R** — Li–Rinzel reduction of de Young–Keizer, Nernst flux.
6. **SERCA** — Purvis/Dode E1/E2 enzymatic cycle.
7. **PMCA** — Caride 5-state, CaM-coupled (basal + Ca₄·CaM-activated paths).
8. **Cytosolic buffering** — CaM ladder (free → Ca₂·CaM → Ca₄·CaM) + gelsolin proxy.
9. **DTS buffering** — CALR / HSP90B1 / BiP / CREC multibuffer.
10. **SOCE** — STIM1 dimerisation + Orai1 MWC, gated on extracellular Ca²⁺.
11. **P2X1 + NCX** — plasma-membrane entry/exit.
12. **Mitochondrial Ca²⁺** — MCU uptake + NCLX release.

**The gap that motivates v0.6:** PLCβ produces both IP₃ *and* DAG. IP₃ drives
the IP3R; **DAG feeds nothing** — it accumulates and is removed only by lumped
DAG kinase. There is no activity-dependent, mechanistic brake on the cascade;
"shut-off" is implicit (ADP unbinding, lumped PAR internalisation, lumped IP₃
degradation).

## Model changes (relative to the earlier v0.4.x core)

- **Kinetics-as-data** (issue #32, phase 2): all calcium rate constants
  externalised to `reports/params/calcium-v0.6.toml` and the 76-species
  inventory to `species-v0.6.tsv`, loaded at import time. (v0.5 used the v0.5
  TOML; v0.6 bumped the default.)
- **Energetics columns**: trapezoidal PMCA ATP cost + an `atp_pump_per_s`
  trace column (SERCA + PMCA ATP consumption). This was a *read-only*
  diagnostic — nothing consumes it back, so it did **not** change trajectories
  (contrast v0.6, which does).

## Results

Validation target — **Dolan & Diamond 2014 Fig. 4**: paired Ca²⁺ transients
with and without extracellular Ca²⁺ (the EDTA/SOCE condition). The model meets
**5/5** criteria — resting cytosol ≈ 100 nM, resting DTS ≈ 250 µM, resting
IP₃ ≈ 50 nM, peak height, and the SOCE differential between the two conditions.
Supporting analyses: a 2-D ADP × thrombin dose sweep (→ Ca²⁺ peak/AUC/rise-time
surfaces) and single-mechanism perturbation knockouts (PMCA V_max rate-limits
cytosolic recovery; MCU buffers cytosolic Ca²⁺ without rescuing the DTS store).

![**v0.5 validation — Dolan & Diamond 2014 Fig. 4.** Cytosolic Ca²⁺ transients under the agonist stimulus with extracellular Ca²⁺ present (SOCE active) versus absent (EDTA condition, SOCE and plasma-membrane leak disabled). The paired traces reproduce the Dolan 5/5 criteria: the resting baseline (~100 nM), the peak height, and the larger sustained plateau when extracellular Ca²⁺ is available for store-operated entry. This is the headline result the calcium core was built to reach, and the baseline that v0.6/v0.61 must preserve.](../figures/v0.5/phase3-dolan-fig4-2026-05-12.png){#fig-v05-dolan width=85%}

![**v0.5 mechanistic perturbation — PMCA V_max rate-limits Ca²⁺ recovery (EDTA transient).** Scaling the PMCA turnover rate (0.25× → 4× baseline) under the no-extracellular-Ca²⁺ condition. Lower PMCA capacity slows the return of cytosolic Ca²⁺ to baseline; higher capacity speeds it. The figure is the template the v0.6 PKC knockouts follow — a single rate constant scanned to expose its functional role.](../figures/v0.5/perturbation-pmca-2026-06-10.png){#fig-v05-pmca width=85%}

---

# v0.6 — PKC negative feedback (closing the DAG branch)

Design: `reports/design/pkc-p2y1-feedback-design-2026-06-11.qmd`.

## Biology

v0.6 adds **PKC** (a single lumped pool standing in for conventional PKCα/β plus
novel PKCδ), activated by the **coincidence of DAG and a rise in cytosolic
Ca²⁺** (Hill cofactor, with a small Ca²⁺-independent floor for the novel,
DAG-only isoform). The gradual accumulation of DAG and Ca²⁺ over the first
~10–15 s supplies a **delay** before the feedback engages, so the brakes shape
the *sustained* phase rather than blunting the peak.

Active PKC drives **two braking feedbacks**:

1. **P2Y1 desensitisation** (the headline; the user's mechanism). PKC
   phosphorylates the *active*, ADP-bound P2Y1 into a signalling-incompetent
   `P2Y1_desensitised[pl]` state that no longer counts toward the Gαq drive.
   ADP-arm specific; grounded in **Mundell 2006** (rapid PKC-dependent P2Y1
   desensitisation in human platelets) and **Nicholas 2023** (a
   desensitisation-resistant P2Y1 raises platelet reactivity).
2. **PLCβ phosphorylation** (the Purvis 2008 route, added on supervisor
   request). PKC moves PLCβ out of the Gq-activatable pool
   (`PLCb_phosphorylated[c]`) — a shared-node brake that pulls IP₃ back toward
   baseline.

These are not mutually exclusive: real PKC does both. Implementing the receptor
route as the headline is a deliberate, better-grounded choice than Purvis's
enzyme-only route, while keeping his route as a validated comparison.

## Model changes

- **State**: 3 new species — `PKC_inactive[c]`, `PKC_active[c]`,
  `P2Y1_desensitised[pl]` (Slice 1); `PLCb_phosphorylated[c]` (Slice 3).
- **Parameters**: new `[pkc.*]` blocks in `calcium-v0.6.toml`; loader default
  bumped v0.5 → v0.6 (v0.5 kept as snapshot).
- **ODE**: PKC activation + the two feedback rate laws added in
  `calcium_signalling.py`'s GPCR/PI-cycle block.
- **Listener**: 3 new `CalciumTrace` columns — `pkc_active`,
  `p2y1_desensitised_frac`, `plcb_phosphorylated_frac`.
- **Experiments**: `runPerturbation.py` `pkc` (knock out `k_des`) and `plcb`
  (knock out `k_plcb_phos`) figures.
- **This is a real biology change, not a no-op**: PKC feeds back into the Ca²⁺
  ODE, so **golden baselines were regenerated and Dolan 5/5 re-verified**
  (preserved — the brakes act late and on the P2Y1 arm / a gently-calibrated
  shared node).

## Results

Because the response is **store-limited**, neither brake changes the cytosolic
Ca²⁺ *amplitude* much in a single transient. The clear, falsifiable readouts are
the mechanism states:

- **P2Y1 desensitised fraction**: PKC knockout (`k_des = 0`) holds it at ~0;
  the baseline feedback drives it to **~0.8** — the loss-of-desensitisation =
  increased-reactivity prediction of Nicholas 2023.
- **IP₃ under the PLCβ brake**: knockout plateaus high; the baseline pulls IP₃
  back toward baseline — the Purvis 2008 "return to baseline" result.

![**v0.6 brake 1 — PKC-mediated P2Y1 desensitisation (ADP-only, +Ca²⁺, 200 s).** ADP-only isolates the P2Y1 arm (thrombin off, so PARs don't mask it). *Left:* cytosolic Ca²⁺ is essentially identical with and without the brake — the response is store-limited, so the receptor brake doesn't move the amplitude. *Right:* the mechanism is unmistakable in receptor state — the baseline feedback desensitises ~80% of active P2Y1 by ~100 s, while the knockout (`k_des → 0`) stays at zero. This is the falsifiable readout against Nicholas 2023.](../figures/v0.6/pkc_traces.png){#fig-v06-pkc width=100%}

![**v0.6 brake 2 — PKC → PLCβ phosphorylation damps IP₃ (Purvis route, standard transient, +Ca²⁺, 300 s).** The PLCβ brake sits on the *shared* node, so the standard thrombin + ADP transient shows it. Knocking out the phosphorylation (`k_plcb_phos → 0`) lets IP₃ plateau high; the baseline feedback sequesters PLCβ out of the Gq-activatable pool and pulls IP₃ back toward baseline — reproducing Purvis 2008. Cytosolic Ca²⁺ stays store-limited.](../figures/v0.6/plcb_traces.png){#fig-v06-plcb width=100%}

![**v0.6 — why the brakes are (almost) invisible on cytosolic Ca²⁺.** *Top:* with brakes on vs off, the two Ca²⁺ traces are essentially superimposed. *Middle:* the DTS store is >98% empty by ~10 s — before the PKC brake engages (~15 s). *Bottom:* the brake then cuts IP₃, but the plateau is set by SOCE (store-depletion gated), not by IP₃ — so cytosolic Ca²⁺ doesn't follow. This timing argument is why the v0.6 readouts are receptor/messenger states, not the Ca²⁺ amplitude, and it sets up the v0.61 second-wave experiment.](../figures/v0.6/why_brake_invisible.png){#fig-v06-why width=72%}

---

# v0.61 — downstream PKC outputs (the amplifiers + the second wave)

Design: `reports/design/pkc-downstream-effects-2026-06-12.qmd`.

## Biology

v0.6 left PKC braking-only. In a real platelet PKC's main job is to drive
**activation outputs**. v0.61 wires the two that feed back into the Ca²⁺ pathway:

- **Granule secretion (Slice 1).** A `GranuleSecretion` process relocates
  pre-existing cargo — dense-granule `ADP[dg]` and `5HT[dg]`, α-granule
  `FGA[ag]` (fibrinogen) → the extracellular space `[e]`, and `SELP[ag]` →
  `SELP_surface[pl]` (the P-selectin activation marker) — first-order in the
  remaining pool, gated by a `PKC_active × Ca²⁺` coincidence term with a
  resting-tone floor, so **resting secretion is exactly zero**.
- **Autocrine ADP loop (Slice 2).** Secreted `ADP[e]` is fed back onto the P2Y1
  drive inside the Ca²⁺ ODE, closing **PKC → secretion → ADP → P2Y1**. The
  pericellular volume `V_EX_L` (~66 fL) is a calibration choice set so full
  dense-granule release ≈ 10 µM. Self-limited by ecto-NTPDase clearance
  (`ADP[e] → AMP[e]`), the v0.6 P2Y1 brake, and finite cargo.
- **Thromboxane synthesis (Slice A).** A `ThromboxaneSynthesis` process lumps
  cPLA₂ → COX-1 → TXA₂-synthase into one Ca²⁺ × PKC-gated production term,
  scaled by the **aspirin knob** `COX1_FACTOR` (0 = aspirin knockout). De-novo
  `TXA2[e]` decays (t½ ≈ 30 s) to the stable ELISA metabolite `TXB2[e]`.
- **Autocrine TXA₂ → TP → Gq (Slice B).** A TP receptor
  (`TP_inactive/active[pl]`) is added to the Ca²⁺ ODE; synthesised TXA₂
  reversibly activates it, and active TP joins the shared `total_active_R` Gq
  drive — the second autocrine amplifier. Aspirin removes the whole loop.

**The unifying picture — PKC closes four loops:**

| Loop | Direction | Route | Added |
|---|---|---|---|
| P2Y1 desensitisation | − brake | PKC → P2Y1 | v0.6 |
| PLCβ phosphorylation | − brake | PKC → PLCβ | v0.6 |
| ADP secretion | + amplify | PKC → dense granule → P2Y1 | v0.61 |
| TXA₂ generation | + amplify | PKC → cPLA₂ → TP → Gq | v0.61 |

The competition between amplification and braking is the mechanistic basis of
the platelet **second wave** — an emergent property no single rate constant
encodes.

## Model changes

- **Processes**: `GranuleSecretion`, `ThromboxaneSynthesis` (the simulation
  group is now RestingDecay, CalciumDynamics, GranuleSecretion,
  ThromboxaneSynthesis).
- **Listeners**: `SecretionTrace` (secreted cargo, released/surface fractions,
  the gate, autocrine `adp_e_uM`) and `ThromboxaneTrace` (`txa2_uM`, `txb2`,
  gate, `tp_active_frac`).
- **State / params**: new species for granule cargo flux, AMP, TXA₂/TXB₂, the TP
  receptor; `[gpcr.tp]` block in the TOML; secretion/thromboxane rate constants.
- **ODE threading**: autocrine `ADP[e]` and `TXA2[e]` threaded into `_ode_rhs`
  via `CalciumDynamics`.
- **Clean toggles** (same live-override pattern as `CA_EX_UM`):
  `calcium_signalling.AUTOCRINE_ADP_GAIN` (1.0 full / 0.0 open-loop) and
  `thromboxane_synthesis.COX1_FACTOR` (aspirin = 0). Used by `runSecondWave.py`.
- **Goldens**: Slice 1/2/A are additive (Ca²⁺ ODE untouched → byte-identical);
  Slice B adds 2 ODE states → goldens regenerated once (`at_rest` perturbed
  ~0.003%, benign solver jitter). **Dolan 5/5 preserved throughout.**

## Results — 1. The modules function

![**v0.61 granule secretion (standard activation).** *Left:* dense-granule cargo (ADP, serotonin) and α-granule cargo (fibrinogen, P-selectin surface exposure) released as a percentage of pool. Release is exactly zero at rest, ramps on activation, and dense-granule release leads α-granule release. *Right:* the `PKC_active × Ca²⁺` secretion gate — zero at rest (PKC below the resting-tone floor), ramping on as the cell activates.](../figures/v0.61/secretion_release.png){#fig-v061-sec width=100%}

![**v0.61 autocrine ADP loop (thrombin-only — zero exogenous ADP).** With no exogenous ADP, PKC-triggered dense-granule secretion is the *only* P2Y1 ligand, so any P2Y1 engagement here is purely autocrine. *Left:* secreted pericellular ADP rises into the P2Y1 range, then ecto-NTPDase hydrolyses it to AMP — the loop self-limits without exhausting the granule pool. *Right:* the resulting P2Y1 desensitisation, driven entirely by autocrine ADP. This is the loop that carries the second wave.](../figures/v0.61/autocrine_adp_loop.png){#fig-v061-adp width=100%}

![**v0.61 thromboxane loop — TXA₂ → TP → Gq (+ aspirin knockout).** *Left:* synthesised TXA₂ reaches ~1 µM and reversibly activates the TP receptor. *Middle:* TP joins the shared Gq drive and modestly amplifies IP₃ versus aspirin (COX-1 = 0, loop off). *Right:* the loop's IP₃ contribution isolated as the difference (intact − aspirin). The effect on IP₃ is modest under strong thrombin (store-limited, PAR-dominated) and negligible on cytosolic Ca²⁺ — the amplifier bites harder at a weak agonist.](../figures/v0.61/thromboxane_loop.png){#fig-v061-tx width=100%}

## Results — 2. Under a saturating agonist the amplifiers don't move Ca²⁺

![**v0.61 amplifiers on vs off, standard saturating agonist, 300 s.** Full v0.61 (autocrine ADP + TXA₂ loops) versus v0.6 (amplifiers off). *Left:* cytosolic Ca²⁺ is unchanged (<0.1%) — store-limited. *Middle:* IP₃ differs a little (~4%) as the amplifiers add Gq drive. *Right:* P2Y1 desensitisation differs clearly (~20%), driven by the autocrine ADP. The amplifiers' effect under a strong agonist lives in the receptor/messenger state and in the new outputs, **not** in cytosolic Ca²⁺.](../figures/v0.61/amplifiers_saturating.png){#fig-v061-sat width=100%}

## Results — 3. The second wave (the capstone result)

The amplifiers bite when the primary stimulus is **weak and transient**, leaving
a recovery phase for them to act on. Thrombin always empties the store
(irreversible PAR cleavage is cumulative → no submaximal regime), so the test
uses **ADP at 0.5 µM, ADP-only, 300 s** (P2Y1 is reversible → graded). Three
conditions: **v0.6** (open loop), **aspirin** (autocrine ADP on, TXA₂ off), and
**full** v0.61.

| Condition | peak Ca²⁺ | Ca²⁺ at 300 s | IP₃ at 300 s | P2Y1 desens. (max) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| v0.6 (open loop) | 313.8 nM | **117.3 nM** | 51.8 nM | 0.19 |
| aspirin (ADP loop on) | 314.1 nM | **210.1 nM** | 189.6 nM | 0.66 |
| full v0.61 | 314.1 nM | **210.1 nM** | 254.9 nM | 0.67 |

- All three reach the **same store-limited peak** (~314 nM).
- In recovery they split: the open loop decays toward baseline (**117 nM**); the
  closed loop stays activated (**210 nM**) — a **+93 nM (~80%) sustained second
  wave** that does not exist in v0.6.
- **Decomposition:** "aspirin" sits exactly on "full" in the Ca²⁺ panel (both
  210.1 nM), so the sustained Ca²⁺ is carried by the **autocrine ADP loop**
  (secreted dense-granule ADP sustains P2Y1 → store stays depleted → SOCE holds
  Ca²⁺ up). TXA₂ adds Gq drive visible in **IP₃** (full 254.9 > aspirin
  189.6 nM) but not in Ca²⁺ (store/SOCE-limited).

This is the classic biology: dense-granule/autocrine amplification and the
aspirin-sensitive second wave matter for *weak* agonists, not saturating ones.

![**v0.61 the second wave — weak transient ADP (0.5 µM, ADP-only, +Ca²⁺ 1.2 mM, 300 s).** Three panels, three conditions. *Left:* all reach the same store-limited peak, then split in recovery — the open-loop model (v0.6) decays toward baseline (117 nM) while the closed-loop model holds ~210 nM (+93 nM sustained second wave). *Middle:* IP₃ — the full model exceeds aspirin, the TXA₂ loop's contribution. *Right:* P2Y1 engagement — the closed loop sustains receptor activity via autocrine ADP. The aspirin and full traces coincide in the Ca²⁺ panel, showing the sustained Ca²⁺ is carried by the autocrine ADP arm, not thromboxane.](../figures/v0.61/second_wave_traces.png){#fig-v061-sw width=100%}

---

# Validation & verification

## Dolan 5/5 across versions

The Dolan & Diamond 2014 Fig. 4 criteria are met by **all three** versions: v0.5
defines the baseline; v0.6's brakes act late / on the P2Y1 arm; v0.61's
amplifiers are additive (Slices 1/2/A) or a tiny perturbation regenerated once
(Slice B). Goldens were regenerated for the genuine biology changes (v0.6 PKC
feedback; v0.61 Slice B) and re-verified.

## What the "5/5" criteria cover — and what they don't

The five implemented criteria (`phase3_dolan_fig4.py`) all probe the **cytosolic
Ca²⁺ peak** and the **SOCE differential** over a 30 s window: peak > 200 nM in
each condition, |peak(+Ca) − peak(−Ca)| ≥ 100 nM, and each peak within ±30% of
the Dolan band. They are a strong **regression guardrail for the calcium core**,
but they are *not* sufficient to validate the v0.6/v0.61 biology, for two
reasons:

1. **The peak is store-limited and occurs before the feedbacks engage** (~10–30 s
   vs the ~15 s PKC delay), so "5/5 preserved" is nearly automatic and is weak
   evidence that the new mechanisms are *correct*.
2. **The recovery phase — where the brakes and the second wave act — is not
   scored.** The reference JSON defines a +Ca sustained-plateau band (200–275 nM)
   that is plotted but not asserted, and the model's plateau under the saturating
   default agonist sits *above* it (~430 nM) — a documented limitation (elevated
   sustained tail; the recovery phase is not yet calibrated to Dolan).

To close the gap, `models/platelet/tests/sim/test_validation_targets.py` adds
**magnitude-band** validations the smoke tests lack: P2Y1 desensitisation reaches
a substantial fraction (≥ 0.5), the PLCβ brake holds IP₃ ≥ 15% below the
unbraked case (Purvis), dense-granule release leads α-granule release and is
substantial, and the second-wave gap lands in a ~+93 nM band with the autocrine
ADP / TXA₂ decomposition intact. The unmet Dolan plateau band is encoded as a
**strict `xfail`** so a future recovery-phase recalibration is flagged
automatically.

## Verification done this session (2026-06-14)

Re-run on the pinned 3.11.5 toolchain (`OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS=1`):

- **Test suite passes** (`models/platelet/tests/`), including the new
  `test_validation_targets.py` (6 passed + 1 strict `xfail` for the Dolan
  plateau gap).
- **mypy: clean** — "no issues found in 57 source files."
- **Second-wave numbers reproduced exactly** from `runSecondWave.py`: peak ~314 nM
  (all conditions), open-loop end 117.3 nM, closed-loop end 210.1 nM, gap
  +92.7 nM, aspirin Ca²⁺ identical to full, full IP₃ > aspirin IP₃ — matching
  the 2026-06-13 lab book.
- All figures in this document were regenerated from the current model via the
  committed scripts (`plotDownstreamModules.py`, `plotStoreLimitedFeedbacks.py`,
  `runPerturbation.py`, `runSecondWave.py`).

---

# References

*Verify journal / volume / DOI in Zotero before thesis use; the entries below
are safe-to-recall anchors (title + first author + approximate year).*

- **Dolan AT, Diamond SL.** Systems modeling of Ca²⁺ homeostasis and mobilization
  in platelets… *Biophys. J.* ~2014. *(The validation target — Fig. 4.)*
- **Purvis JE, Chatterjee MS, Brass LF, Diamond SL.** A molecular signaling model
  of platelet phosphoinositide and calcium regulation… *Blood.* 2008;112:4069.
  *(PLCβ-phosphorylation brake; SERCA/PMCA kinetics.)*
- **Mazet F, Tindall MJ, Gibbins JM, Fry MJ.** A model of the PI cycle… *Sci.
  Rep.* 2020;10. *(PI-cycle framework; note the 13889/13244 volume discrepancy
  flagged in the v0.6 design doc.)*
- **Mundell et al.** Distinct roles for protein kinase C isoforms in regulating
  platelet purinergic receptor function. *Mol. Pharmacol.* 2006. *(Primary
  evidence for PKC-mediated P2Y1 desensitisation.)*
- **Nicholas et al.** Loss of P2Y1 receptor desensitization… increased platelet
  reactivity in vitro. 2023. *(Falsifiable validation target for the v0.6 brake.)*
- **Golebiewska EM, Poole AW.** Platelet secretion: from haemostasis to wound
  healing… *Blood Rev.* ~2015. *(Granule-secretion review.)*
- **FitzGerald GA.** Mechanisms of platelet activation: thromboxane A₂ as an
  amplifying signal. *Am. J. Cardiol.* ~1991. *(TXA₂ autocrine amplification.)*

*Design docs:* `pkc-p2y1-feedback-design-2026-06-11.qmd` (v0.6),
`pkc-downstream-effects-2026-06-12.qmd` (v0.61). *Lab book:*
`lab-book-2026-06-13-second-wave.md`.
